Under the impugned amended Rules, linkage of Aadhaar numbers to\n\n        bank accounts is mandatory and persons not enrolling for Aadhaar\n\n        cannot operate a bank account, which violates the spirit of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1228\">Article 14</a> in\n\n        entirety in so far it arbitrarily metes out unequal treatment based on\n\n        unreasonable classification;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1106\">     b The impugned Rules are violative of <a href=\"/doc/935769/\" id=\"a_1229\">Article 19(1)(g)</a> as the Rules refer\n\n        to companies, firms, trusts, etc., whereas the Aadhaar Act is only to\n\n        establish identity of \u201cindividuals\u201d;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1107\">     c Non-operation of a bank account, even for a temporary period, leads\n\n        to deprivation of an individual&#x27;s property and therefore constitutes a\n\n        violation under <a href=\"/doc/120077007/\" id=\"a_1230\">Article 300A</a> of the Constitution, which provides that\n\n        deprivation can be done only by primary legislation; and\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_786\">                                         39</span>\n\f                                                                             PART C\n\n\n       d The Rule has no nexus to the object of the <a href=\"/doc/1697463/\" id=\"a_1231\">PMLA Act</a>, as the Act has\n\n            no provision to make bank accounts non-operational;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1108\">ii    <a href=\"/doc/190513775/\" id=\"a_1232\">Section 139AA</a> of the Income Tax Act, 1961 is liable to be struck down as\n\n      violative of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1233\">Articles 14</a><a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1234\">, 21</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_1235\">19(g)</a> of the Constitution;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1109\">iii   The decision in Binoy Viswam v Union of India49 requires re-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1110\">      consideration in view of the nine judge Bench decision in Puttaswamy;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1111\">iv    In view of serious deficiencies in the Aadhaar Act, there is a need for\n\n      guidelines under <a href=\"/doc/500307/\" id=\"a_1236\">Article 142</a> to protect inter alia, the right to privacy and to\n\n      implement the mandate of the nine judge Bench in Puttaswamy;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1112\">v     If the Aadhaar project is not struck down, it should be confined only for\n\n      identification or authentication of persons who are entitled to subsidies,\n\n      benefits and services for which expenditure is incurred from the\n\n      Consolidated Fund of India;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1113\">vi    Sections 2(g), 2(j) 7, 57 and 59 of the Aadhaar Act violate <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1237\">Articles 14</a><a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1238\">, 21</a>\n\n      and <a href=\"/doc/120077007/\" id=\"a_1239\">300A</a> of the Constitution; and\n\n\nvii   PMLA Rule 9 is arbitrary as it is contrary to the RBI Master Circular\n\n      (issued in 2013), which provided a list of documents that were to be\n\n      treated as \u2018identity proof\u2019, in relation to proof of name and proof of\n\n      residence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1114\">49(2017)   7 SCC 59\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_787\">                                         40</span>\n\f                                                                          PART C\n\n\n\n\nMr P Chidambaram, learned Senior Counsel argued that the Aadhaar Act\n\ncould not have been passed as a Money Bill. Thus, he submitted:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1115\">i     The only difference between financial bills and money bills is the term\n\n      \u201conly\u201d in <a href=\"/doc/72095/\" id=\"a_1240\">Article 110</a> of the Constitution which implies that the scope of\n\n      money bills is narrower than the scope of financial bills and provisions\n\n      relating to money bills must thus be construed strictly;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1116\">ii    The Aadhaar Act, which was passed as a money bill, should be struck\n\n      down since many of its provisions such as <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1241\">Section 57</a> have no relation to\n\n      the nature of a Money Bill and bear no nexus to the Consolidated Fund of\n\n      India;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1117\">iii   Since Money Bills can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha, on account of\n\n      the curtailment of the powers of the Rajya Sabha and the President, the\n\n      relevant provisions must be accorded a strict interpretation;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1118\">iv    While <a href=\"/doc/1753411/\" id=\"a_1242\">Article 110(3)</a> provides that the decision of the Speaker of the Lok\n\n      Sabha as to whether a Bill is a \u2018Money Bill\u2019 shall be final, the finality is\n\n      only with regard to the Parliament and does not exclude judicial review;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1119\">      and\n\n\nv     Since the legislative procedure is illegal and the power of the Rajya\n\n      Sabha has been circumvented to disallow legislative scrutiny of the\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_788\">                                        41</span>\n\f                                                                         PART C\n\n\n     Aadhaar bill, provisions of the Act cannot be severed to save the Act and\n\n     the Act is liable to be struck down as a whole by the Court.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1120\">Mr KV Vishwanathan, learned Senior Counsel made the following\n\nsubmissions:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1121\">i    All acts done prior to the passage of the Act are void ab initio and are not\n\n     saved or validated by <a href=\"/doc/638929/\" id=\"a_1243\">Section 59</a>. In any event, <a href=\"/doc/638929/\" id=\"a_1244\">Section 59</a> is invalid;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1122\">ii   Collection, storage and use of data under the Aadhaar project and Act\n\n     are invalid for the following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1123\">     a. The Aadhaar Act and the surrounding infrastructure has made the\n\n        possession of Aadhaar de facto mandatory;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1124\">     b. Compulsory collection of identity information violates various facets of\n\n        the right to privacy - bodily privacy, informational privacy and\n\n        decisional autonomy;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1125\">     c. <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_1245\">The Act</a> is unconstitutional since it collects the identity information of\n\n        children between 5-18 years without parental consent;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1126\">     d. Centralised storage of identity information and the unduly long period\n\n        of retention of transaction data and authentication records is\n\n        disproportionate;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_789\">                                        42</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1127\">\f                                                                         PART C\n\n\n      e. <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_1246\">The Act</a> and Regulations preclude Aadhaar number holders from\n\n         accessing or correcting their identity information stored on the CIDR;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1128\">         and\n\n      f. <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_1247\">The Act</a> and Regulations lack safeguards to secure sensitive personal\n\n         data.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1129\">iii   Services like health related services, and those related to food, pensions\n\n      and daily wages claimed under <a href=\"/doc/1849490/\" id=\"a_1248\">Section 7</a> of the Act have been denied\n\n      because of biometric failure. Biometric infrastructure operates on a\n\n      probabilistic system, which cannot be \u2018one hundred percent infallible\u2019.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1130\">      Thus, the State needs to take steps to prevent the denial of benefits by\n\n      adopting alternate methods for verification of identity. This is absent at\n\n      present, resulting in a violation of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1249\">Articles 14</a> <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1250\">&amp; 21;</a>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1131\">iv    No provision is made for a hearing against omission and deactivation of\n\n      the Aadhaar number, which violates the principles of natural justice; and\n\n\nv     Sections 2(g), 2(j), 2(k) and 23(2) of the Aadhaar Act suffer from\n\n      excessive delegation and the allied regulations are vague, manifestly\n\n      arbitrary and unreasonable.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1132\">Mr Anand Grover, learned Senior Counsel has submitted thus:\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_790\">                                          43</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1133\">\f                                                                           PART C\n\n\ni     The Aadhaar project extends far beyond the scope of the Aadhaar Act\n\n      with no procedural safeguards. Hence it violates <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1251\">Article 21</a> in as much as\n\n      it is without the support and sanction of law. The data collected is\n\n      unauthorised, excessive and being illegally shared;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1134\">ii    The use of biometric technology to establish identity is uncertain,\n\n      unproven and unreliable leading to exclusion and a violation of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1252\">Articles 14</a>\n\n      and <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1253\">21;</a>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1135\">iii   The lack of security in the Aadhaar project violates the right to privacy\n\n      under <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1254\">Article 21;</a>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1136\">iv    Excessive powers have been delegated to the UIDAI through the\n\n      Aadhaar Act; and\n\nv     <a href=\"/doc/1881144/\" id=\"a_1255\">Sections 33(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1256\">57</a> of the Act are vague, overbroad and\n\n      constitutionally invalid.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1137\">Ms Meenakshi Arora, learned Senior Counsel contended that:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1138\">i     The general and indiscriminate retention of personal data, including meta-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1139\">      data, and the ensuing possibility of surveillance by the State has a chilling\n\n      effect on fundamental rights like the freedom of speech and expression,\n\n      privacy, and dignity;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_791\">                                        44</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1140\">\f                                                                            PART C\n\n\nii    Making Aadhaar the sole means of identification for various services\n\n      impinges upon dignity as it amounts to requiring a license for the exercise\n\n      of fundamental rights; and\n\n\niii   The Aadhaar project does not contain any specific provisions for data\n\n      protection, apart from a mere general obligation on UIDAI, which is a\n\n      violation of the obligation of the State to ensure that the right to life,\n\n      liberty, dignity and privacy of every individual is not breached under Part\n\n      III of the Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1141\">Mr Sajjan Poovayya, learned Senior Counsel has urged the following\n\nsubmissions:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1142\">i     The Aadhaar Act fails to satisfy the constitutional test of a just, fair and\n\n      reasonable law;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1143\">ii    Maintenance of Aadhaar records by the State under <a href=\"/doc/179995/\" id=\"a_1257\">Section 32</a> is an\n\n      unwarranted intrusion by the State;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1144\">iii   Use of personal information under <a href=\"/doc/1670053/\" id=\"a_1258\">Section 33</a> is an unwarranted intrusion\n\n      by the State;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1145\">iv    <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1259\">Section 57</a> of the Act is contrary to the principle of purpose limitation; and\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_792\">                                         45</span>\n\f                                                                         PART C\n\n\nv    <a href=\"/doc/137331837/\" id=\"a_1260\">Sections 2(g)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1261\">2(j)</a>, the proviso to <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1262\">Section 3(1)</a>, <a href=\"/doc/472995/\" id=\"a_1263\">Section 23(2)(g)</a> and\n\n     <a href=\"/doc/472995/\" id=\"a_1264\">Section 23(2)(n)</a> read with <a href=\"/doc/85373501/\" id=\"a_1265\">Section 54(2)(l)</a>, and <a href=\"/doc/176471/\" id=\"a_1266\">Section 29(4)</a> of the Act\n\n     suffer from the vice of excessive delegation.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1146\">Mr CU Singh, learned Senior Counsel, argued that the rights of the child are\n\nviolated through the Aadhaar project. A child has no right to give consent or to\n\nenter into a contract. A child in India, under law, has no power or right to bind\n\nherself to anything, to consent or enter into contracts. In this background,\n\nthere is no compelling state interest to mandate Aadhaar for children. The\n\nfundamental right of a child to education cannot be made subject to production\n\nof Aadhaar. These requirements are not only contrary to domestic legislation\n\nprotecting the rights of children but also against India\u2019s international\n\nobligations. Learned counsel also spoke of the violation of the rights of\n\nhomeless people who are denied benefits due to the lack of a fixed abode.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1147\">Mr Sanjay Hegde, learned Senior Counsel has urged that since there is no\n\n\u2018essential practice\u2019 involved, exemptions must be allowed from the mandatory\n\nnature of the Aadhaar Act on the grounds of freedom of conscience under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/631708/\" id=\"a_1267\">Article 25</a> of the Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1148\">Ms Jayna Kothari, learned Counsel arguing on behalf of an intervenor\n\norganization for transgender persons and sexual minorities urged that the\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_793\">                                       46</span>\n\f                                                                        PART C\n\n\nAadhaar Act discriminates against sexual minorities. Aadhaar Regulations\n\nrequire demographic information. The enrolment form has a third gender, but\n\nthere is no uniformity across the board, and the documents that have to be\n\nproduced to get an Aadhaar card do not always have that option. Aadhaar is\n\nbeing made mandatory for almost everything but transgender persons cannot\n\nget an Aadhaar because they do not have the gender identity documents that\n\nAadhaar requires. This non-recognition of gender identity leads to denial of\n\nbenefits which is violative of both <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1268\">Articles 14</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1269\">21.</a>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1149\">It has also been argued before us in an intervention application that denial of\n\nAadhaar to Non-Resident Indians leads to discrimination when NRIs seek to\n\navail of basic services in India.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1150\">C.2    Respondents\u2019 submissions\n\n\nMr KK Venugopal, Learned Attorney General for India, has submitted thus:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1151\">i.    For the period prior to coming into force of the Aadhaar Act, because of\n\n      the interim orders passed by the SC, obtaining an Aadhaar number or\n\n      enrollment number was voluntary, and hence there was no violation of\n\n      any right;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1152\">ii.   Section 59 of the Aadhaar Act protects all actions taken from the period\n\n      between 2010 till the passage of the Aadhaar Act in 2016;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_794\">                                        47</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1153\">\f                                                                          PART C\n\n\n\niii.   The judgments in MP Sharma and Kharak Singh being those of 8 and 6\n\n       judges respectively, holding that the right to privacy is not a fundamental\n\n       right, judgments of smaller benches delivered during the period upto\n\n       Puttaswamy would be per incuriam. Hence, the State need not have\n\n       proceeded on the basis that a law was required for the purpose of getting\n\n       an Aadhaar number or an enrolment number. As a result, the\n\n       administrative actions taken would be valid as well as the receipt of\n\n       benefits and subsidies by the beneficiaries;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1154\">iv.    Subsequent to the Aadhaar Act, the petitioners would have to establish\n\n       that one or more of the tests laid down by the nine judge bench in\n\n       Puttaswamy render the invasion of privacy resulting from the Aadhaar\n\n       Act unconstitutional. The tests laid down in Puttaswamy have been\n\n       satisfied and hence the Aadhaar Act is not unconstitutional for the\n\n       following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1155\">       a. The first condition in regard to the existence of a law has been\n\n          satisfied;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1156\">       b. Legitimate state interests such as preventing the dissipation of social\n\n          welfare benefits, prevention of money laundering, black money and\n\n          tax evasion, and protection of national security are satisfied through\n\n          the Act;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_795\">                                         48</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1157\">\f                                                                        PART C\n\n\n  c. The Aadhaar Act satisfies the test of proportionality by ensuring that a\n\n     \u201crational nexus\u201d exists between the objects of the Act and the means\n\n     adopted to achieve its objects; and\n\n  d. For the purpose of testing legitimate State interest and proportionality,\n\n     the Court must take note of the fact that each one of the subsidies and\n\n     benefits under <a href=\"/doc/1849490/\" id=\"a_1270\">Section 7</a> is traceable to rights under <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1271\">Article 21</a> of the\n\n     Constitution - such as the right to live with human dignity, the right to\n\n     food, right to shelter, right to employment, right to medical care and\n\n     education. If these rights are juxtaposed with the right to privacy, the\n\n     former will prevail over the latter.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1158\">v. The Aadhaar Act was validly passed as a Money Bill on the following\n\n  grounds:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1159\"> a. The term \u2018targeted delivery of subsidies\u2019 contemplates an expenditure of\n\n    funds from the Consolidated Fund of India, which brings the Aadhaar\n\n    Act within the purview of a Money Bill under <a href=\"/doc/72095/\" id=\"a_1272\">Art. 110</a> of the Constitution;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1160\"> b. <a href=\"/doc/1849490/\" id=\"a_1273\">Sections 7</a>, <a href=\"/doc/47944/\" id=\"a_1274\">24</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1226169/\" id=\"a_1275\">25</a> and the Preamble of the Act also support its\n\n    classification as a Money Bill;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1161\"> c. The Aadhaar Act has ancillary provisions, but they are related to the\n\n    pith and substance of the legislation which is the targeted delivery of\n\n    subsidies and benefits; and\n\n d. <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1276\">Section 57</a> of the Act is saved by <a href=\"/doc/1542332/\" id=\"a_1277\">Article 110 (1) (g)</a> of the Constitution as\n\n    it is a standalone provision and even if a Bill is not covered under\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_796\">                                      49</span>\n\f                                                                               PART C\n\n\n          clauses (a) to (f) of Article110(1), it can still be covered under Article110\n\n          (1) (g).\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1191\">Mr Tushar Mehta, learned Additional Solicitor General, submitted:\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1162\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"115701246\" id=\"span_797\">i.      <a href=\"/doc/190513775/\" id=\"a_1278\">Section 139AA</a> of the Income Tax Act, was examined in Binoy Viswam\n\n        in the context of <a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_1279\">Article 19</a> and fulfills the three tests laid down under\n\n        Puttaswamy as well as the test of manifest arbitrariness laid down in\n\n        <a href=\"/doc/115701246/\" id=\"a_1280\">Shayara Bano v Union of India50</a>;</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1163\">ii.     The demographic information that is required for Aadhaar enrollment is\n\n        already submitted while obtaining a PAN card and therefore individuals\n\n        do not have a legitimate interest in withholding information;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1164\">iii.    Linking Aadhaar to PAN is in public interest on the following grounds:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1165\">        a. The State has a legitimate interest in curbing the menace of black\n\n             money, money laundering and tax evasion, often facilitated by\n\n             duplicate PAN cards, and the linking of Aadhaar to the PAN card will\n\n             ensure that one person holds only one PAN Card, thereby curbing\n\n             these economic offences;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1166\">        b. Aadhaar-PAN linking is in public interest and satisfies the test of\n\n             proportionality and reasonableness;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1167\">50   (2017) 9 SCC 1\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_798\">                                            50</span>\n\f                                                                    PART C\n\n\nc. The individual interest gives way to a larger public interest and a\n\n   statutory provision furthering state interest will take precedence over\n\n   fundamental rights;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1192\">d. The Court must not interfere with the Legislature\u2019s wisdom unless the\n\n   statutory measure is shockingly disproportionate to the object sought\n\n   to be achieved;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1193\">e. India is a signatory to various international treaties under which it has\n\n   obligations to take action to curb the menace of black money and\n\n   money laundering in pursuance of which measures including the\n\n   amendments to inter alia the Income Tax Act and the PMLA Act and\n\n   Rules thereunder, have been brought about by the legislature;\n\nf. Statutory provisions under Aadhaar Act and Income Tax Act are distinct\n\n   and standalone. Moreover, the validity of one provision cannot be\n\n   examined in the light of the other;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1194\">g. Ascribing a (mandatory or voluntary) character to the provisions of a\n\n   statute is Parliament\u2019s prerogative and cannot be questioned by\n\n   courts; and\n\nh. Rule 9 of the amended PMLA Rules that mandates furnishing of an\n\n   Aadhaar number to open a bank account is not ultra vires the Aadhaar\n\n   Act. Similarly, the Rule that an existing bank account will become non-\n\n   operational if not linked with Aadhaar within six months is not a\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_799\">                                  51</span>\n\f                                                                            PART C\n\n\n          penalty but a consequence to render the accounts of money\n\n          launderers non-operational.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1195\">\n\n\nMr Rakesh Dwivedi, learned Senior Counsel, has submitted:\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1168\">i.     The right to privacy exists when there is a reasonable expectation of\n\n       privacy. However, this reasonable expectation of privacy differs from one\n\n       dataset to another since the Aadhaar Act draws a distinction between\n\n       demographic information, optional demographic information (eg. mobile\n\n       number), core biometric information (fingerprints and iris scans) and\n\n       biometric information such as photographs;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1169\">ii.    Alternatively, the applicability of <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1281\">Article 21</a> has to be confined and limited\n\n       to core biometric information;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1170\">iii.   Fundamental rights are not absolute and can be restricted if permitted\n\n       specifically. <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1282\">Article 21</a> expressly envisages deprivation by laws which\n\n       seek to carry out legitimate objectives and are reasonable and\n\n       proportionate;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1171\">iv.    The Aadhaar Act does not cause exclusion because if authentication fails\n\n       after multiple attempts, then the subsidies, benefits and services, can be\n\n       availed of by proving the possession of an Aadhaar number, either by\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_800\">                                         52</span>\n\f                                                                           PART C\n\n\n      producing the Aadhaar card or by producing the receipt of the application\n\n      for enrolment and producing the enrolment ID number;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1172\">v.    Section 7 of the Aadhaar Act protects the right to human dignity\n\n      recognized by <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1283\">Article 21</a> of the Constitution by providing services,\n\n      benefits and subsidies. The Aadhaar Act is a welfare scheme in\n\n      pursuance of the State\u2019s obligation to respect the fundamental rights to\n\n      life and personal liberty; to ensure justice (social, political and economic)\n\n      and to eliminate inequality (<a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1284\">Article 14</a>) with a view to ameliorate the lot of\n\n      the poor and the Dalits;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1173\">vi.   Socio-economic rights must be read into Part III of the Constitution since\n\n      civil and political rights cannot be enjoyed without strengthening socio -\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1174\">      economic rights;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1175\">vii. A welfare State has a duty to ensure that each citizen has access at least\n\n      to the basic necessities of life. The idea of a socialist state under a\n\n      mandate to secure justice-     social, economic     and political - will   be\n\n      completely illusory if it fails to secure for its citizens the basic\n\n      necessities in life.   There cannot be any dignity for those who suffer\n\n      starvation, subjugation, deprivation and marginalization and those who\n\n      are compelled to do work which is intrinsically below human dignity;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_801\">                                        53</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1176\">\f                                                                            PART C\n\n\nviii. The Aadhaar number does not convert individuals to numbers. The\n\n      Aadhaar number is necessary for authentication and it is solely used for\n\n      that purpose. The petitioners have conflated the concepts of identity and\n\n      identification. Authentication through a number is merely a technological\n\n      requirement which does not alter the identity of an individual;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1177\">ix.   Even if there is a conflict between the right to privacy and the right to food\n\n      and shelter, the Aadhaar Act strikes a fair balance. The Aadhaar Act\n\n      ensures human dignity and the right to life and liberty, hence there would\n\n      be no reasonable expectation of privacy and autonomy;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1178\">x.    The requirement to obtain an Aadhaar number under the Aadhaar Act\n\n      does not reflect a lack of trust in citizens. Authentication by the State\n\n      does not presume that all its citizens are dishonest. The provisions of the\n\n      Aadhaar Act are merely regulatory in nature - similar to the process of\n\n      frisking at airports or other offices - since there is no effective method to\n\n      ensure targeted delivery;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1179\">xi.   The \u201cleast intrusive test\u201d is not applicable in the present case. The\n\n      requirement that the least intrusive means of achieving the State object\n\n      must be adopted, has been rejected by Indian courts in a catena of\n\n      decisions as it involves a value judgment and second guessing the\n\n      wisdom of the legislature. Such a test violates the separation of powers\n\n      between the legislature and the judiciary;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_802\">                                         54</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1180\">\f                                                                        PART C\n\n\nxii. Even assuming that the \u2018least intrusive method\u2019 test applies, the exercise\n\n    of determining the least intrusive method of identification is a technical\n\n    exercise and cannot be undertaken in a court of law;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1181\">xiii. The Petitioners who have furnished smartcards as an alternative to the\n\n    Aadhaar card, have not established that smartcards are less intrusive\n\n    than the Aadhaar card authentication process;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1182\">xiv. The \u2018strict scrutiny test\u2019 does not apply to the Aadhaar Act. That test is\n\n    conceptualised in the United States, to be only applied to \u2018suspect\n\n    classifications\u2019;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1183\">xv. Section 7 of the Aadhaar Act does not involve any waiver of fundamental\n\n    rights;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1184\">xvi. There can be no assumption of mala fide against the government or the\n\n    legislature. A mere possibility of abuse is not a ground to invalidate the\n\n    Aadhaar Act;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1185\">xvii. Through <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1285\">Section 57</a>, Parliament intended to make the use of the Aadhaar\n\n    number available for other purposes due to the liberalization and\n\n    privatization of the economy in areas earlier occupied by the government\n\n    and public sector. Many private corporate bodies are operating parallel to\n\n    and in competition with the public sector such as in banking, insurance,\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_803\">                                      55</span>\n\f                                                                          PART C\n\n\n    defence, and health. These are core sectors absolutely essential for\n\n    national integrity, to the national economy and the life of people;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1186\">xviii. <a href=\"/doc/137331837/\" id=\"a_1286\">Sections 2(g)</a>, and (j) read with <a href=\"/doc/85373501/\" id=\"a_1287\">Section 54(2)(a)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1030207/\" id=\"a_1288\">Section 54(1)</a> do not\n\n    suffer from excessive delegation of power to UIDAI and there are\n\n    sufficient guidelines coupled with restrictions. The regulation making\n\n    power of the Authority under the Act is limited by the use of the\n\n    expression \u2018such other biological attribute\u2019 which will be interpreted\n\n    ejusdem generis with the categories of information mentioned before\n\n    namely, fingerprints and iris scan. These categories have certain\n\n    characteristics: firstly, they do not contain genetic information; secondly,\n\n    they are non-intrusive; thirdly, apart from carrying out authentication they\n\n    do not reveal any other information of the individual; fourthly, these are\n\n    modes of identification used for identifying a person even without digital\n\n    technology; fifthly, they are capable of being used for instantaneous\n\n    digital authentication; and sixthly, they are biological attributes enabling\n\n    digital authentication. The addition of biological attributes, under <a href=\"/doc/1030207/\" id=\"a_1289\">Section\n\n    54</a>, must mandatorily be laid before the Parliament under <a href=\"/doc/1704110/\" id=\"a_1290\">Section 55</a>. This\n\n    is an additional check on the regulation making power of UIDAI;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1187\">xix. Under <a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1291\">Section 2(k)</a>, which defines demographic information, certain\n\n    sensitive categories of information such as \u2018race, religion, caste, tribe,\n\n    ethnicity, language, records of entitlement, income or medical history\u2019 of\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_804\">                                       56</span>\n\f                                                                        PART C\n\n\n    the person are excluded. The term \u2018other relevant information\u2019 has to be\n\n    construed ejusdem generis and would have to be necessarily\n\n    demographic in nature as contrasted with biometric information;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1188\">xx. Aadhaar is necessary, as 3% of India\u2019s GDP amounting to trillions of\n\n    rupees is allocated by Governments towards subsidies, scholarships,\n\n    pensions,     education,   food   and   other    welfare   programmes.   But\n\n    approximately half of if does not reach the intended beneficiaries.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1189\">    Aadhaar is necessary for fixing this problem as no other identification\n\n    document is widely and commonly possessed by the residents of the\n\n    country and most of the other identity documents do not enjoy the quality\n\n    of portability;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1190\">xxi. The enrolment and authentication processes under the Aadhaar Act are\n\n    strongly regulated so that the data is secure;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1191\">xxii. The security of the CIDR is also ensured through adequate measures and\n\n    safeguards;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1192\">xxiii. The Aadhaar Act ensures that UIDAI has control over the requesting\n\n    entity during the authentication process;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1193\">xxiv. Enrolment Regulations ensure that the requirement of informed consent\n\n    of individuals is fulfilled while securing the Aadhaar card in the following\n\n    ways:\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_805\">                                       57</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1194\">\f                                                                        PART C\n\n\n    a. Firstly, the resident is given an opportunity of verifying his or her\n\n       information for accuracy before uploading;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1195\">    b. Secondly, the details and the supporting documents are provided by\n\n       the resident, or an introducer (in specific cases);\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1196\">    c. Thirdly, the enrolling agency is obliged to inform the individual about\n\n       the manner in which the information shall be used, the nature of\n\n       recipients with whom the information is to be shared during\n\n       authentication; and the existence of a right to access information, the\n\n       procedure for making request for such access and details of the\n\n       person/ department to whom a request can be made; and\n\n    d. Fourthly, the uploading of information is done in the presence of the\n\n       individual.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1197\">xxv. When an individual makes a choice to enter into a relational sphere then\n\n    his or her choice as to mode of identification would automatically get\n\n    restricted on account of the autonomy of the individuals or institution with\n\n    whom they wish to relate. This is more so where the individual seeks\n\n    employment, service, subsidy or benefits;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1198\">xxvi. The Central government has the power to direct the linking of Aadhaar\n\n    card, with SIM card, as it is proportional to the object sought to be\n\n    achieved in the interest of national security;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_806\">                                       58</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1199\">\f                                                                           PART C\n\n\nxxvii. Regarding the process of authentication and metadata retained under\n\n       the Act, it is submitted:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1200\">    a. The only purpose of the Aadhaar project is authentication and there is\n\n        no power under the Act to analyze data;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1201\">    b. The Aadhaar Act does not involve big data or learning algorithms. It\n\n        merely utilizes a matching algorithm for the purpose of authentication;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1202\">    c. Metadata contemplated is process or technical metadata and does not\n\n        reveal anything about the individual. <a href=\"/doc/95109658/\" id=\"a_1292\">Section 2(d)</a> of the Act defines\n\n        \u201cauthentication   record\u201d   to   mean   the   record   of   the   time   of\n\n        authentication, identity of the RE and the response provided by the\n\n        Authority\u201d, and the relevant authentication regulation, <a href=\"/doc/1762415/\" id=\"a_1293\">Regulation 26</a>,\n\n        does not go beyond the scope of <a href=\"/doc/95109658/\" id=\"a_1294\">Section 2(d)</a> of the Act;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1203\">    d. Moreover, <a href=\"/doc/1762415/\" id=\"a_1295\">Regulation 26</a> and <a href=\"/doc/179995/\" id=\"a_1296\">Section 32(3)</a> of the Act prohibit the\n\n        Authority from collecting or storing any information about the purpose\n\n        of authentication; and\n\n    e. Only limited technical metadata is required to be stored in an effort to\n\n        exercise control over REs by way of audits.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1204\">xxviii. Regarding the security of the Aadhaar data, it is submitted:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1205\">    a. The provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1965344/\" id=\"a_1297\">Information Technology Act, 2000</a> and the\n\n        punitive measures provided there are made applicable to Aadhaar\n\n        data under Section 30 of the Aadhaar Act; and\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_807\">                                         59</span>\n\f                                                                          PART C\n\n\n    b. Anyone attempting to gain unauthorized access to the CIDR faces\n\n       stringent punishment, including imprisonment upto 10 years.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1206\">xxix. On the control exercised by the Authority over the Requesting Entities\n\n     (RE), the following was urged before the Court:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1207\">     a. The standard of control exercised by the Authority on the Requesting\n\n        Entities is \u2018fair and reasonable\u2019 as laid down under <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1298\">Article 21</a> of the\n\n        Constitution;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1208\">    b. This control includes requirements that the RE\u2019s procure the\n\n       fingerprint device from vendors controlled by the Authority, with the\n\n       Authority also providing the hardware and software of the device. The\n\n       device is subject to quality checks, and must be certified by the\n\n       Authority before being used by the RE. The Authority also takes\n\n       measures to ensure that data is sent to it in an encrypted form;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1209\">    c. The license is given to the RE from the Authority only after an audit of\n\n       the RE is conducted, and the audit report is approved; and\n\n    d. The data collected by these REs is segregated and there exists no\n\n       way of aggregating this data. During authentication requests, the full\n\n       identity information of the individual will never be transmitted back to\n\n       the REs by the Authority as there exists a statutory bar from sharing\n\n       Biometric information under <a href=\"/doc/176471/\" id=\"a_1299\">Sections 29 (1) (a)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/176471/\" id=\"a_1300\">29(4)</a> of the Act.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_808\">                                      60</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1210\">\f                                                                            PART C\n\n\nxxx. UIDAI has entered into licensing agreements with foreign biometric\n\n    solution providers (BSP) for software. Even though the source code of the\n\n    software is retained by the BSP as it constitutes their intellectual property,\n\n    the data in the server rooms is secure as the software operates\n\n    automatically and the biometric data is stored offline. There is no\n\n    opportunity available to the BSP to extract data as they have no access\n\n    to it;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1211\">xxxi. Prior to the enactment of the Aadhaar Act, the Aadhaar project was\n\n    governed by the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/1965344/\" id=\"a_1301\">Information Technology Act, 2000</a>.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1212\">    <a href=\"/doc/69360334/\" id=\"a_1302\">Section 72A</a> of the Information Technology Act, 2000 provides for\n\n    punishment for disclosure of information in breach of law or contract;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1213\">xxxii. The architecture of the Aadhaar Act does not enable any real possibility,\n\n    proximate or remote, of mass surveillance in real time by the State;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1214\">xxxiii. The giving of identity information and undergoing authentication has no\n\n    direct and inevitable effect on <a href=\"/doc/1378441/\" id=\"a_1303\">Article 19(1)(a).</a> Alternatively, even if <a href=\"/doc/1378441/\" id=\"a_1304\">Article\n\n    19(1)(a)</a> is attracted, <a href=\"/doc/493243/\" id=\"a_1305\">Article 19(2)</a> would protect Section 7 of the Aadhaar\n\n    Act as it has a direct and proximate nexus to public order and security of\n\n    the State;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1215\">xxxiv. In response to the argument that the requirements of Aadhaar number\n\n    and authentication for benefits, services and subsidies would be ultra\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_809\">                                        61</span>\n\f                                                                         PART C\n\n\n    vires <a href=\"/doc/1419768/\" id=\"a_1306\">Article 243-G</a> and items 11, 12, 16, 17, 23, 25 and 28 of the XIth\n\n    Schedule, it is submitted that the Panchayats get only such powers as\n\n    are given to it by the legislature of the State. <a href=\"/doc/1419768/\" id=\"a_1307\">Article 243-G</a> is merely\n\n    enabling. There is no compulsion upon the State to endow the\n\n    Panchayats with powers relating to the items specified in the XIth\n\n    Schedule;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1216\">xxxv. On the validity and purpose of <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1308\">Section 57</a>, it is urged:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1217\">    a. <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1309\">Section 57</a> is not an enabling provision. It merely provides, as it states,\n\n        that the provisions of the Act would not prevent the use of Aadhaar for\n\n        other purposes;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1218\">    b. However, <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1310\">Section 57</a> imposes a limitation on any such use for other\n\n        purposes, that the use must be sanctioned by any law in force or any\n\n        contract;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1219\">    c. Another limitation is presented by the proviso to <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1311\">Section 57</a>, which\n\n        says that the use of the Aadhaar number shall be subject to the\n\n        procedure and obligations under <a href=\"/doc/971685/\" id=\"a_1312\">Section 8</a> and Chapter VI, which\n\n        would necessarily also subject it to the operation of Chapter VII\n\n        (dealing with Offences &amp; Penalties) of the Act;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1220\">    d. Under <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1313\">Section 57</a>, the State, a body corporate or any other person\n\n        cannot become Requesting Entities unless the limitations provided for\n\n        under <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1314\">Section 57</a> are complied with;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_810\">                                       62</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1221\">\f                                                                            PART C\n\n\n    e. <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1315\">Section 57</a> imposes limitations, and the use is backed by\n\n        authentication, protection of information and punitive measures;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1222\">    f. The expressions \u2018pursuant to any law or any contract\u2019, and \u2018to this\n\n        effect\u2019- necessarily entail that where the State makes a law or any\n\n        body corporate enters into a contract, the law or contract should be\n\n        prior in point of time to the making of any application for becoming a\n\n        Requesting Entity or a Sub-Authentication User Agency under\n\n        <a href=\"/doc/1843082/\" id=\"a_1316\">Regulation 12</a> of the Authentication Regulations; and\n\n    g. A large number of small service providers simply cannot become\n\n        Requesting Entities under <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1317\">Section 57</a>, as they will not meet the\n\n        rigorous standard demanded by the eligibility conditions which are\n\n        prescribed by the Regulations to become Authentication User\n\n        Agencies (AUA)/ KYC User Agencies (KUA). Therefore, this provision\n\n        does not create a situation whereby the common man is required to\n\n        undergo authentication in all activities.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1223\">xxxvi. The Aadhaar Act is not exclusionary but inclusionary since it provides\n\n      all citizens the bare necessities for a dignified existence;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1224\">xxxvii. Having the option to opt-out is not a constitutional requirement.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1225\">Mr Neeraj Kishan Kaul, learned Senior Counsel, made the following\n\nsubmissions:\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_811\">                                        63</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1226\">\f                                                                          PART C\n\n\n\ni.     Aadhaar is a speedy and reliable tool for identification and authentication\n\n       and there is no reason to hold it invalid;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1227\">ii.    Private entities and AUAs/KUAs that have built their businesses around it\n\n       should be allowed to use Aadhaar authentication services;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1228\">iii.   <a href=\"/doc/174899/\" id=\"a_1318\">Section 57</a> is an enabling provision and private players should be given\n\n       the choice to use the Aadhaar authentication services as a tool for\n\n       verification if there is a consensus between private players\u2019 and their\n\n       customers;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1229\">iv. Aadhaar authentication has benefited women in villages and migrants and\n\n       increased the reach of microfinance institutions, thus reducing predatory\n\n       financing; and\n\nv.     A statute cannot be struck down on the ground that there is scope for\n\n       misuse.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1230\">Mr Jayant Bhushan, learned Senior Counsel appearing for the Reserve Bank\n\nof India urged the following submissions before the Court:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1231\">i.     RBI, in exercise of its powers under the <a href=\"/doc/1129081/\" id=\"a_1319\">Banking Regulation Act, 1949</a>\n\n       and Rule 9 of the PMLA Rules, 2005 issued an amended Master Circular\n\n       on April 20, 2018 which mandates that Aadhaar has to be submitted to a\n\n       Reporting Entity. This circular conforms with the PMLA rules;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_812\">                                          64</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1232\">\f                                                                          PART C\n\n\nii.    Rule 9(14) of the PMLA Rules provides that the Regulator- the RBI in this\n\n       case, lay down guidelines incorporating the requirements of sub-rules\n\n       9(1)-(13), which would include enhanced or simplified measures to verify\n\n       identity; and\n\niii. The requirement of submission of Aadhaar to the RE is in exercise of this\n\n       power under Rule 9(14).\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1233\">Mr Gopal Sankarnarayanan, learned counsel, has submitted:\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1234\">i.     The Aadhaar Act as a whole does not violate the fundamental right to\n\n       privacy;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1235\">ii.    The factors that save the Aadhaar Act from failing the proportionality test\n\n       are (a) Voluntariness to subject one \u2019s identity information to obtain\n\n       the Aadhaar ; (b) Informed consent when such identity information is\n\n       utilized; and (c) A draw on the Consolidated Fund of India;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1236\">iii.   Right to identity is a fundamental right as a part of the right to dignity,\n\n       which is being realized by the Aadhaar Act;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1237\">iv.    The right to identity is also recognized under India\u2019s international\n\n       obligations under instruments such as the UDHR and ICCPR;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1238\">v.     In view of the large scale enrolments that have already taken place and\n\n       the expenditure incurred by the Government out of public funds, it would\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_813\">                                         65</span>\n\f                                                                             PART C\n\n\n      be in overarching public interest to give <a href=\"/doc/638929/\" id=\"a_1320\">Section 59</a> full effect. If this were\n\n      not done, the only avenue available to the Government would be to\n\n      undertake the mammoth enrolment task all over again under a new\n\n      regime, affording only a pyrrhic victory to the Petitioners, while there\n\n      would   be    substantial   revenue     losses   to   the Government      and\n\n      deprivation of beneficial schemes to those eligible, in the meanwhile;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1239\">vi.   Certain provisions of the Aadhaar Act have to be struck down or read\n\n      down so that the Act as a whole can continue to serve its essential\n\n      purpose - namely <a href=\"/doc/454297/\" id=\"a_1321\">Sections 47</a>, <a href=\"/doc/971685/\" id=\"a_1322\">Section 8(4)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/176471/\" id=\"a_1323\">Section 29(2)</a> of the Act;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1240\">      and\n\n\n\nvii. <a href=\"/doc/190513775/\" id=\"a_1324\">Section 139AA</a> of the Income Tax Act, 1961 violates <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1325\">Article 14</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1326\">21</a> of\n\n      the Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1241\">Mr Zoheb Hossain, learned Counsel, made the following submissions:\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1242\">i.    The right to privacy cannot be asserted vicariously on behalf of others in\n\n      a representative capacity in a Public Interest Litigation, because unlike\n\n      other constitutional rights, right to privacy is a personal right. No <a href=\"/doc/1849490/\" id=\"a_1327\">Section 7</a>\n\n      beneficiary has claimed a violation of their right to privacy despite the\n\n      pendency of the petitions for 6 years before this Court and therefore, the\n\n      Petitioners&#x27; challenge, in a representative capacity, to <a href=\"/doc/1849490/\" id=\"a_1328\">section 7</a> on the\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_814\">                                         66</span>\n\f                                                                           PART C\n\n\n        ground of a violation of the right to privacy of third parties is not\n\n        maintainable;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1243\">ii.     There is no increased threat to privacy due to Aadhaar at the level of\n\n        requesting entities (RE) for the following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1244\">        a. REs are already in possession of personal information of individuals\n\n            and inclusion of Aadhaar does not in any manner increase the threat\n\n            to privacy;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1245\">        b. Any information disclosed by REs will not be on account of Aadhaar\n\n            and will have to be dealt with under domain specific legislations, or a\n\n            data protection regime or agreements between the REs and their\n\n            customers; and\n\n        c. REs have data of their own customers and not of other REs\u2019\n\n            customers, so there is no possibility of surveillance.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1246\">iii.    Safeguards against disclosure of information in the Aadhaar Act are\n\n        superior to the safeguards <a href=\"/doc/115701246/\" id=\"a_1329\">laid down in</a> the PUCL case51. <a href=\"/doc/971685/\" id=\"a_1330\">Sections 8</a>, <a href=\"/doc/555776/\" id=\"a_1331\">28</a>\n\n        and <a href=\"/doc/176471/\" id=\"a_1332\">29</a> along with Chapter VII which deals with Offences and Penalties,\n\n        provide for protection of information and <a href=\"/doc/1670053/\" id=\"a_1333\">Section 33</a> lays down a strict\n\n        procedure for disclosure. Even though the Aadhaar Act is not required to\n\n        meet the same standard as <a href=\"/doc/115701246/\" id=\"a_1334\">laid down in</a> PUCL, the safeguards in the Act\n\n        are not only adequate with regard to identity information and\n\n        authentication records, but far exceed the safeguards laid down PUCL;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1247\">51   (2011) 14 SCC 331\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_815\">                                           67</span>\n\f                                                                             PART C\n\n\n\niv.   The petitioners cannot contend that Section 33(2) of the Aadhaar Act\n\n      goes against the principles of natural justice and is disproportionate (as it\n\n      does not define the term \u201cnational security\u201d) for the following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1248\">      a. What is in the interest of \u201cnational security\u201d is not a question of law but\n\n         that of policy lying in the executive domain; and\n\n      b. Principles of natural justice cannot be observed strictly in a situation\n\n         implicating national security. In such cases, it is the duty of the court to\n\n         read into and provide for statutory exclusion.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1249\">v.    The laws, which are under challenge, are a part of a concerted scheme to\n\n      promote redistributive justice and ensure substantive equality, in\n\n      furtherance of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1335\">Articles 14</a><a href=\"/doc/1673816/\" id=\"a_1336\">, 38</a>, 39B and 39C. These laws ensure a more\n\n      transparent and a cleaner system, root out revenue leakages and evasion\n\n      of taxes, thereby giving genuine beneficiaries their rightful share in\n\n      subsidies;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1250\">vi.   The object of the Aadhaar Act, contrary to what the petitioners have\n\n      argued, is totally unrelated to suppression of freedom of speech and any\n\n      incidental effect, if at all, would not implicate the right under <a href=\"/doc/1378441/\" id=\"a_1337\">Article\n\n      19(1)(a);</a>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1251\">vii. The petitioners cannot contend that Section 47 of the Aadhaar Act is\n\n      arbitrary or unreasonable for the following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_816\">                                         68</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1252\">\f                                                                         PART C\n\n\n    a. The offences and penalties under the Act are intended to maintain the\n\n       purity of data of the Aadhaar number holder and the integrity of the\n\n       CIDR, which are integral in achieving the object of the Act;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1253\">    b. Enrolment, storage of data in CIDR, and authentications are so vast\n\n       and inherently technical that any breach of the provisions, can be\n\n       effectively dealt with by the UIDAI;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1254\">    c. The individual has not been left remediless, as he/she can make a\n\n       complaint to the UIDAI directly or through the grievance redressal\n\n       centre [<a href=\"/doc/179995/\" id=\"a_1338\">Regulation 32</a> of the Aadhaar (Enrolment and Update)\n\n       Regulations, 2016]. After a complaint has been made, the UIDAI\n\n       would be obliged to examine the complaint and accordingly lodge a\n\n       complaint in a Court in terms of Section 47 of the Aadhaar Act;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1255\">    d. Section 56 of the Aadhaar Act makes it clear that application of other\n\n       laws, like the <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_1339\">IT Act</a>, is not barred.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1256\">viii. Aadhaar must be made mandatory under Section 7 of the Aadhaar Act for\n\n    the following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1257\">    a. Because of the involvement of biometrics, it is almost impossible for\n\n       one person to obtain two Aadhaar numbers. This will help in checking\n\n       the entry of fake and duplicate beneficiaries into any welfare scheme;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1258\">    b. Other methods which were employed over the last 70 years to check\n\n       duplication, siphoning of money in welfare schemes, large-scale tax\n\n       evasion, generation of black money, and appearance and re-\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_817\">                                        69</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1259\">\f                                                                           PART C\n\n\n        appearance of duplicates, have turned out to be futile. If Aadhaar is\n\n        made voluntary, the same problems are likely to creep back into the\n\n        system; and\n\n     c. The State is bound to deploy the best technology available to it to\n\n        ensure proper allocation of resources as there is a constitutional\n\n        mandate upon the State under <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1340\">Article 14</a> to efficiently utilize its\n\n        resources.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1260\">ix. There is no conflict between the Aadhaar Act and the Income Tax Act as\n\n     they are both stand alone laws and their scope of operation is different;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1261\">x.   Through the Aadhaar Act, the State is furthering the following obligations\n\n     under Part III and Part IV of the Constitution and international obligations:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1262\">     a. The State has a positive obligation for securing socio-economic rights\n\n        like the basic right to food, shelter and livelihood of people arising out\n\n        of <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1341\">Article 21</a>, even though it is worded negatively;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1263\">     b. The Supreme Court has observed that civil &amp; political rights and socio-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1264\">        economic rights in India are placed on the same pedestal [PUCL].\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1265\">        Aadhaar is a means of achieving the latter set of rights. The\n\n        proportionality analysis would therefore require a balancing of rights in\n\n        this context;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_818\">                                        70</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1266\">\f                                                                                              PART C\n\n\n         c. <a href=\"/doc/1673816/\" id=\"a_1342\">Articles 38</a><a href=\"/doc/1200546/\" id=\"a_1343\">, 39(b)</a>, (c), (e), (f)<a href=\"/doc/1975922/\" id=\"a_1344\">, 41</a><a href=\"/doc/1256023/\" id=\"a_1345\">, 43</a><a href=\"/doc/1551554/\" id=\"a_1346\">, 47</a> and <a href=\"/doc/346437/\" id=\"a_1347\">51(c)</a> impose a\n\n             constitutional mandate on the State to ensure effective and efficient\n\n             utilization of public resources;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1267\">         d. The State is the trustee of public resources towards people, and\n\n             inaction of the State to plug the continuous leakage of public\n\n             resources and revenues would violate both, the principle of non-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1268\">             arbitrariness and reasonableness envisaged by <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1348\">Article 14</a> as well as\n\n             the constitutional doctrine of public trust; and\n\n         e. The creation of Aadhaar infrastructure and enactment of the Aadhaar\n\n             Act is a step towards the government pursuing India\u2019s international\n\n             obligations under the ICESCR.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1269\">xi. While testing proportionality, reasonableness of a restriction has to be\n\n         determined in an objective manner from the standpoint of the interests of\n\n         the general public and not from the perspective of an individual right\n\n         bearer claiming invasion52; and\n\n\nxii. With regard to the alleged conflict between Section 29(2) of the Aadhaar\n\n         Act and <a href=\"/doc/13503/\" id=\"a_1349\">Section 4(b)(xii)</a> of the RTI Act, the former cannot be struck down\n\n         as unconstitutional for the following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1270\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"185766757\" id=\"span_819\">52   <a href=\"/doc/185766757/\" id=\"a_1350\">Modern Dental College and Research Centre v State of Madhya Pradesh</a>, (2016) 7 SCC 353.</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_820\">                                                     71</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1196\">\f                                                                         PART D\n\n\n\n     a. A provision can be struck down only if it is in violation of the\n\n        Constitution or if the legislature lacks competence, not on the ground\n\n        that it is in conflict with another law;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1197\">     b. In any case, the obligations of public authorities under both these\n\n        provisions are different, as the public authority under the <a href=\"/doc/671631/\" id=\"a_1351\">RTI Act</a> can\n\n        publish the details of beneficiaries from the existing database and the\n\n        information received by the UIDAI is not required to be shared or\n\n        displayed publicly. However, if any information is displayed publicly, it\n\n        can be challenged by an aggrieved person on the ground of privacy\n\n        which would be completely unrelated to the present challenge;\n\n     c. The two laws operate in their distinct fields and there is no conflict\n\n        between them; and\n\n     d. A conflict between two statutes is required to be reconciled through\n\n        harmonious construction. However, since there is no conflict between\n\n        these two laws, there is no need for harmonious construction.\n\n\n\nD     Architecture of Aadhaar: analysis of the legal framework\n\n\n39    The architecture of the Aadhaar Act envisages the creation of a unique\n\nidentity for residents on the basis of demographic and biometric information.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_1352\">The Act</a> envisages a process of identification by which the unique identity\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_821\">                                          72</span>\n\f                                                                                                      PART D\n\n\nassigned to each individual is verified with the demographic and biometric\n\ninformation pertaining to that individual which is stored in a centralised\n\nrepository of data known as the Central Identities Data Repository (CIDR). The\n\nformer part of the legislative design is implemented by its regulatory provisions\n\ngoverning enrolment53 of individuals who would be allotted a unique identity\n\nnumber. The latter part of the legislative design consists of the process of\n\n\u2018authentication\u2019.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1198\">\n\n40         In order to facilitate an understanding of the key aspects of the law,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1353\">Section 2</a> provides a dictionary of meanings. \u2018Aadhaar number\u2019 is defined in\n\n<a href=\"/doc/151628761/\" id=\"a_1354\">Section 2(a)</a> as the identification number issued to the individual under sub-\n\nsection (3) of <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1355\">Section 3</a>. The individual to whom an Aadhaar number is issued\n\nis described in <a href=\"/doc/32617707/\" id=\"a_1356\">Section 2(b)</a> as the \u2018Aadhaar number holder\u2019. The expression\n\n\u2018authentication\u2019 is defined in <a href=\"/doc/126067998/\" id=\"a_1357\">Section 2(c)</a> thus:\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1271\">                    \u201c(c) \u201cAuthentication\u201d means the process by which the\n                    Aadhaar number alongwith demographic information or\n                    biometric information of an individual is submitted to the\n                    Central Identities Data Repository for its verification and such\n                    Repository verifies the correctness or lack thereof, on basis of\n                    information available with it.\u201d\n\n\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/95109658/\" id=\"a_1358\">Section 2(d)</a> speaks of the \u2018authentication record\u2019 as the record of the time of\n\nauthentication, the identity of the requesting entity and the response provided\n\nby UIDAI. The crucial definitions are those of \u2018biometric information\u2019, \u2018core\n\n53    <a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1359\">Section 2(m)</a> states: \u201cenrolment\u201d means the process, as may be specified by regulations, to collect\n     demographic and biometric information from individuals by the enrolling agencies for the purpose of issuing\n     Aadhaar numbers to such individuals under this Act.\n</blockquote>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_822\">                                                       73</span>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1199\">\f                                                                                 PART D\n\n\nbiometric information\u2019, \u2018demographic information\u2019 and \u2018identity information\u2019.\n\nThese are as follows:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1272\">            \u201c(g) \u201cbiometric information\u201d means photograph, finger print,\n            Iris scan, or other such biological attributes of an individual as\n            may be specified by regulations;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1273\">            ...\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1274\">            (j) \u201ccore biometric information\u201d means finger print, Iris scan, or\n            such other biological attribute of an individual as may be\n            specified by regulations;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1275\">            (k) \u201cdemographic information\u201d includes information relating to\n            the name, date of birth, address and other relevant\n            information of an individual, as may be specified by\n            regulations for the purpose of issuing an Aadhaar number,\n            but shall not include race, religion, caste, tribe, ethnicity,\n            language, records of entitlement, income or medical history.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1200\">            ...\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1201\">            (n) \u201cidentity information\u201d in respect of an individual, includes\n            his Aadhaar number, his biometric information and his\n            demographic information.\u201d\n\n\n\nThe largest subset of the above definitions consists of \u2018identity information\u2019\n\nwhich is defined in an inclusive sense to comprehend the Aadhaar number,\n\nbiometric information and demographic information. Demographic information\n\nis defined as information related to the name, date of birth and address and\n\nother information pertaining to an individual as is specified by the regulations.\n\nSignificantly, <a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1360\">Section 2(k)</a> excludes, by a mandate, race, religion, caste, tribe,\n\nethnicity, language, records of entitlement, income or medical history from the\n\npurview of demographic information. Biometric information consists, under\n\n<a href=\"/doc/137331837/\" id=\"a_1361\">Section 2(g)</a>, of the photograph, fingerprint, Iris scan, or other such biological\n\nattributes of an individual as may be specified by regulations. Core biometric\n\ninformation in <a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1362\">Section 2(j)</a> excludes photographs (which form part of biometric\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_823\">                                             74</span>\n\f                                                                               PART D\n\n\ninformation). Apart from photographs, other biometric information is\n\ncomprehended within core biometric information and may be expanded to\n\ninclude other biological attributes specified in the regulations to be made\n\nunder the Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1202\">\n\n41         The identity information of an individual is stored in a central depository.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/41060201/\" id=\"a_1363\">Section 2(h)</a> defines \u201cCentral Identities Data Repository\u201d as a centralised\n\ndatabase in one or more locations containing all Aadhaar numbers issued to\n\nAadhaar number holders along with the corresponding demographic\n\ninformation and biometric information of such individuals and other related\n\ninformation. The CIDR is the backbone of the Aadhaar Act. All the information\n\ncollected or created under the Act is stored in it. For the establishment and\n\nmaintenance of the CIDR, it has been provided54 under the Act that UIDAI may\n\nengage one or more entities, which can also perform any other functions as\n\nmay be specified by regulations. <a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_1364\">The Act</a> does not prohibit the engagement of\n\nprivate entities for the establishment and maintenance of the CIDR.\n\n\n\n42         <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1365\">Section 3</a>, pertains to the entitlement to obtain an \u2018Aadhaar Number\u2019,\n\nwhich forms a part of Chapter II titled \u2018enrolment\u2019. <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1366\">Section 3</a> comprises of\n\nthree parts: (i) an entitlement of every resident to obtain an Aadhaar number;\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1203\">(ii) a requirement of submitting demographic and biometric information to be\n\nenrolled; and (iii) a process of undergoing enrolment. <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1367\">Section 3</a> provides thus:\n\n\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1276\">54   Section 10, Aadhaar Act\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_824\">                                            75</span>\n\f                                                                                                         PART D\n\n\n                     \u201cSection (3): Aadhaar Number.-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1277\">                     (1) Every resident shall be entitled to obtain an Aadhaar\n                        number by submitting his demographic information\n                        and biometric information by undergoing the process\n                        of enrolment:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1278\">                        Provided that the Central Government may, from time\n                        to time, notify such other category of individuals who\n                        may be entitled to obtain an Aadhaar number.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1279\">                     (2) The enrolling agency shall, at the time of enrolment,\n                        inform the individual undergoing enrolment of the\n                        following details in such manner as maybe specified\n                        by regulations, namely:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1280\">                         (a) The manner in which the information shall be\n                             used;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1281\">                         (b) The nature of recipients with whom the information\n                             is intended to be shared during authentication; and\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1282\">                         (c) The existence of a right to access information, the\n                             procedure for making requests for such access\n                             and details of the person or department in-charge\n                             to whom such requests can be made.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1283\">                     (3) On receipt of the demographic information and\n                        biometric information under sub-section (1), the\n                        Authority shall, after verifying the information, in such\n                        manner as may be specified by regulations, issue an\n                        Aadhaar number to such individual.\u201d\n\n\n\n\nSignificantly, sub-section (1) of <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1368\">Section 3</a> recognises an entitlement, of every\n\nresident55 to obtain an Aadhaar number. An entitlement postulates a right. A\n\nright contemplates a liberty, for it is in the exercise of the liberty that the\n\nindividual asserts a right. What is a matter of an entitlement is evidently a\n\nmatter of option and not a compulsion. That constitutes the fundamental\n\npostulate of <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1369\">Section 3</a>. However, the entitlement to obtain the Aadhaar\n\n\n\n\n55   <a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1370\">Section 2(v)</a> states: \u201cresident\u201d means an individual who has resided in India for a period or periods amounting\n     in all to one hundred and eighty-two days or more in the twelve months immediately preceding the date of\n     application for enrolment\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_825\">                                                         76</span>\n\f                                                                                                       PART D\n\n\nnumber is conditioned by the requirement of submitting demographic and\n\nbiometric information and participating in the process of enrolment.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1204\">43       The collection of demographic and biometric information is carried out\n\nby an enrolling agency. \u201cEnrolling agency\u201d has been defined under <a href=\"/doc/545792/\" id=\"a_1371\">Section\n\n2(l)</a> of the Act as an agency, appointed by UIDAI or a Registrar56, for collecting\n\ndemographic and biometric information of individuals under the Act. The\n\nenrolling agency need not be an entity of the state. The definition opens the\n\nspace for engagement of private entities in the collection of individual\n\ninformation for the process of enrolment. The enrolling agencies have to set\n\nup enrolment centers and they have to function in accordance with the\n\nprocedure specified by UIDAI.57 Sub-section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/694023/\" id=\"a_1372\">Section 3</a> requires the\n\nenrolling agency to disclose to the individual, who is undergoing enrolment,\n\nthree important facets. The first is the manner in which the information which\n\nis disclosed by the individual would be used. The second relates to the nature\n\nof the recipients with whom the information is likely to be shared during the\n\ncourse of authentication. The third is founded upon the individual\u2019s right of\n\naccess to the information disclosed. All these three facets are crucial to the\n\nlegislative design because they try to place individual autonomy at the\n\nforefront of the process.
